Location: West 167th Street, Orland Park, Illinois, US (41.586, -87.896)

Checklist Comments: FluddlesIL    Joined Jayne Leone here when I arrived, and she provided some context for where her white-headed shorebird had disappeared to. She left and Ryan Jones arrived and looked with me for a while. We eventually found the bird, and concluded it was an aberrant likely leucistic Lesser Yellowlegs.

FluddlesIL    This area is spectacular and I’m super excited to have it as one of my sites. While I didn’t see any shorebirds or waterfowl during my visit today, the fields here are completely flooded. There’s the Engel road Fluddle of course but there are also plenty of other little fluddles in the surrounding fields. Once we get closer to peak shorebird migration, I think we’ll see a lot of amazing birds here. Highly recommend a scope to view the far side of the fluddle, although binoculars were alright.    Habitat description: Large fluddle surrounded by significant flooding in nearby fields that has created other fluddles. There aren’t currently extensive mudflats at the site and the water seems relatively high.     Partly cloudy  52 degrees  6 mph winds
FluddlesIL    No shorebirds other than Killdeer

Location: Fluddle next to Kaneland High School
Observation Details: *Rare. Continuing find by Scott Cohrs. A gorgeous Limosa godwit molting into alternate plumage with a black and white underwing, splotchy barring of rufous underparts, bright pale supercilium, pale bases to the inner primary shafts, white rump with black terminal tail base.     Given the preponderance of fluddles due to historic rains, this has been one of the best Springs for Hudsonian Godwits in recent memory. I planned on nabbing the Chapman SWHA and knew it would likely be easier in the late morning, so Scott's HUGO report was a perfect primer for the birding day. One of my favorite birds and first time enjoying it in flight. Great meeting Aaron and Bill and connecting with Ann.
Checklist Comments: Cold NW wind, feels like 20, overcast, soft drizzle ending as I arrived.    FluddlesIL

Observation Details: Pic

Three birds flew in and landed about 10 minutes after I arrived. The long, bicolored, slightly upturned bills and chestnut bellies were immediately obvious. Later, when they flushed and circled, the black underwings and black tails/white rumps were clearly visible. Photos to be added.

*Rare. Continuing find by Ryan Jones earlier today. Large shorebirds with upturned, bicolored bill and rufous belly. My first HUGOs in alternate plumage. Absolutely stunning to experience in person. Wish I could have spent more time with them.
Continuing. Found by Ryan Jones. Photos. Birds in breeding plumage. Unmistakable with long, decurved, bicolored bill.

*Rare. Only third county record on eBird, plus a fourth known record of one bird that occurred briefly on April 13 photographed at this same location by Suzy Bidggood (posted on Facebook). This flock of three was spotted today by Ryan Jones, who saw them fly in this morning. Gorgeous large shorebirds, two of which were in alternate plumage and the third which was still mostly in basic. Shorebirds with long bicolored bills with a slight upturned tip, pink/orange bases with black tips, white superciliums, gray faces and streaked necks, brownish/gold-speckled backs, gray wings, and in the alternate plumaged birds, had rich chestnut/reddish underparts and black and white barred undertails. White rumps with black tail tips, and black underwings. Photos and videos taken.

Checklist Comments: FluddlesIL    This area is spectacular and I’m super excited to have it as one of my sites. While I didn’t see any shorebirds or waterfowl during my visit today, the fields here are completely flooded. There’s the Engel road Fluddle of course but there are also plenty of other little fluddles in the surrounding fields. Once we get closer to peak shorebird migration, I think we’ll see a lot of amazing birds here. Highly recommend a scope to view the far side of the fluddle, although binoculars were alright.    Habitat description: Large fluddle surrounded by significant flooding in nearby fields that has created other fluddles.
